summary: Amy is a second-year criminology student who presents as refreshingly honest and unpretentious, frequently saying 'I don't know' before giving thoughtful responses. She has a strong home-cooking identity, eating at home 90% of the time and finding cooking therapeutic for managing anxiety. Her food world revolves around pasta as a safe, versatile staple, though she's quite texture-sensitive and will go off foods for weeks if they don't taste right. She lives in a house with five others but maintains individual food habits, recently transitioning from delivery grocery shopping to in-person trips with a housemate who drives. Amy positions herself as someone who rarely orders takeaways, claiming once a month or less, yet shows sophisticated knowledge of delivery apps, promotions, and student deals. Her delivery behaviour is entirely socially driven - she never initiates orders but readily joins when housemates suggest it, treating it as a social bonding activity rather than a food solution. The Valentine's Day order she describes (getting £15 worth of food for £2 each due to a 70% discount) reveals someone who gets genuinely excited about bargains and sees deals as the primary justification for delivery orders.
personality_notes: Conversational and unpretentious, frequently admits when she doesn't know something. Self-aware about habits (like high social media usage). Responds thoughtfully after initial uncertainty. Seems genuine rather than trying to impress.
food_relationship: Home cooking is central to her identity and stress management, with pasta as her reliable go-to, though she's quite particular about textures and will avoid foods that don't taste right.

key_moments.description: Describes cooking as stress relief and anxiety management
key_moments.significance: Reveals cooking serves an emotional regulation function, not just sustenance

key_moments.description: Valentine's Day takeaway story - got 70% off, paid £2 each instead of £15
key_moments.significance: Shows genuine excitement about deals and how promotions can completely change her delivery behaviour

key_moments.description: Explains takeaway ordering as 'if others want one, can I join in please?'
key_moments.significance: Reveals delivery is purely social for her - never self-initiated, always follower behaviour

what_not_said: Never mentioned financial stress despite being a student with no job
Didn't discuss any cooking failures or food waste
No mention of dietary restrictions beyond texture preferences
Avoided discussing why she cancelled Uber One subscription in detail
initial_hypotheses: Her 'rare' takeaway usage may be understated - she shows too much platform knowledge for truly infrequent use
Social dynamics are the primary driver for delivery orders, not convenience or hunger
Deal-seeking behaviour suggests price sensitivity but she doesn't explicitly discuss budget constraints
Her cooking-as-therapy approach may make delivery feel like 'giving up' on self-care
questions_for_coding: How does her social follower behaviour around delivery relate to other decision-making patterns?
What's the real frequency of delivery orders versus her stated 'once a month or less'?
How does her texture sensitivity and food pickiness influence restaurant/cuisine choices?
Does her cooking-as-stress-relief identity create guilt or conflict around ordering delivery?
